title: Common Beginner Mistakes
summary: Avoid the traps every beginner falls into

steps.title: The Big Five
steps.text:
Every player loses the same handful of games as a beginner. Skip these mistakes and you'll skip a thousand losses.

## The five classic blunders

1. **Moving the same piece twice in the opening.** Every tempo wasted is a piece your opponent develops for free.
2. **Forgetting to castle.** A king in the center invites tactics — even strong middlegames collapse without a tucked-away king.
3. **Bringing the queen out too early.** She gets chased by minor pieces, and every retreat is a move you didn't develop.
4. **Playing automatic moves.** Recapturing without thinking, defending a pawn that wasn't attacked, copying the opponent — autopilot is fatal.
5. **Playing without a plan.** A move with no purpose is a wasted move. Every move should improve a piece, threaten something, or prepare a break.

A classic example: after **{{e4}} {{e5}} {{Qh5}}?!**, White hopes for a quick attack. Black just plays **{{Nc6}}** and **{{Nf6}}** — and the queen has to retreat while Black develops with tempo.

> The opening is a race. Develop, don't dance.

steps.title: Don't Grab Every Pawn
steps.text:
Pawn-grabbing is the most seductive trap in chess. *It's free material!* — except, often, it isn't.

In the position above, White has just played the Center Game with **{{e4}} {{e5}} {{d4}}**. If Black greedily grabs with {{exd4}}, White recaptures, develops with tempo, and gets a big lead in piece activity.

## Three questions before any pawn snatch

- **Will I have to move the same piece again?** If yes, you're losing tempo.
- **Will my opponent develop a piece with check or threat while recapturing?** Every check costs you another move.
- **Will my king or king's pawn cover get exposed?** A weak king is rarely worth a single pawn.

A pawn is one point. **Three tempos** of development is also worth roughly one point. So *every* pawn grab that costs three tempos is a losing trade.

> Take pawns when they're free, not when they're bait.

steps.title: Watch Your Opponent's Threats
steps.text:
Half of all losing positions come from a single source: ignoring what the opponent just did.

When your opponent moves, they have a **reason**. Maybe they're attacking a piece. Maybe they're opening a file. Maybe they're vacating a square for a knight. Find the reason — *before* you choose your own move.

## The opponent's-move scan

- *What did this move attack?* (Anything new under fire?)
- *What did this move defend?* (Are they preparing a follow-up?)
- *What did this move open?* (A new diagonal, file, or rank?)
- *What did this move threaten?* (A check next move? A fork?)

If you can't answer, look longer. The threat is usually there.

## A real-world example

If your opponent plays **{{Bg5}}**, the obvious threat is the pin on your knight. The subtle threat is doubling your pawns after **{{Bxf6}}**, or — if you've castled — opening lines toward your king.

> Your move begins by understanding theirs.
